jokes.text: Or does that say 'applesauce'?
jokes.setup: Hank is trying to read applause signs to warm up the audience
jokes.punchline: Misreads 'applause' as 'applesauce' - a malapropism
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank bumbling - character-appropriate mistake that plays on visual similarity of words

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Or does that say 'applesauce'?

jokes.text: The only way we'll talk is if I'm booked on the show as a guest. Fine. I'll book you. You're on. Except then you can't do Arsenio for the next three to six months
jokes.setup: Jeannie sarcastically suggests the only way to talk is if she's booked as a guest
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately agrees but adds the contractual restriction about not doing competing shows
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Larry's show-business mind immediately kicks in even during marital crisis. Perfect integration of his obsessive industry mindset

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: The only way we'll talk is if I'm booked on the show as a guest.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Fine. I'll book you. You're on. Except then you can't do Arsenio for the next three to six months

jokes.text: Five, four, three, two... - What does that mean?
jokes.setup: Deep personal conversation interrupted by show countdown
jokes.punchline: Larry's confused response to the countdown in midst of crisis
jokes.explanation: Perfect collision of professional and personal worlds - Larry momentarily disoriented by show business routine during emotional moment

dialogue.speaker: Stage Manager
dialogue.line: Five, four, three, two...

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What does that mean?

jokes.text: Marilyn Quayle had the only one that said, 'I'm with stupid.'
jokes.setup: Larry talking about various buttons at Republican convention
jokes.punchline: Marilyn Quayle wearing 'I'm with stupid' button (referring to Dan Quayle)
jokes.explanation: Solid political joke playing on Dan Quayle's reputation - good structure but fairly predictable target

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Marilyn Quayle had the only one that said, 'I'm with stupid.'

jokes.text: Dr. Ruth is just back from Africa. I'll book her tomorrow night. It's not a sexual problem, Artie.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining intimacy problems with wife
jokes.punchline: Artie immediately assumes it's sexual and suggests Dr. Ruth
jokes.explanation: Artie's practical show-biz solution to personal problems - character-appropriate response

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Dr. Ruth is just back from Africa. I'll book her tomorrow night.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: It's not a sexual problem, Artie.

jokes.text: I remember when you were making those toaster waffles, you were very happy. Don't tell me that I was happy. I was not happy. I haven't been happy for weeks, including yesterday when I made the toaster waffles, which, by the way, you like.
jokes.setup: Larry cites toaster waffles as evidence of Jeannie's happiness
jokes.punchline: Jeannie's escalating denial ending with the detail that he likes the waffles
jokes.explanation: Excellent marital argument logic - Larry's clueless observation vs Jeannie's sharp rebuttal with the cutting detail

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I remember when you were making those toaster waffles, you were very happy.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: Don't tell me that I was happy. I was not happy. I haven't been happy for weeks, including yesterday when I made the toaster waffles, which, by the way, you like.

jokes.text: Chicago gets my attention 'cause I like that city and it has a very distinctive-sounding name. I did Second City in Chicago, too, so I have fond memories. I did Second City mostly in Toronto, but I also... Chicago reminds me of Toronto.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to explain why he overheard their conversation
jokes.punchline: His rambling, self-absorbed justification that makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank - turning someone else's crisis into his own reminiscence, making the situation more awkward

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Chicago gets my attention 'cause I like that city and it has a very distinctive-sounding name. I did Second City in Chicago, too, so I have fond memories. I did Second City mostly in Toronto, but I also... Chicago reminds me of Toronto.

jokes.text: I did Second City in Chicago, too, so I have fond memories. I did Second City mostly in Toronto, but I also...
jokes.setup: Hank continuing to explain his Chicago interest
jokes.punchline: Adding unnecessary personal comedy credentials to justify eavesdropping
jokes.explanation: Continuation of Hank's self-important rambling. Character consistent but less punchy than the setup.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I did Second City in Chicago, too, so I have fond memories. I did Second City mostly in Toronto, but I also...

jokes.text: They get you for 8 minutes, and I get you for 90 seconds. That's pretty much the problem in a nutshell.
jokes.setup: Larry saying he'll be back in 8 minutes to talk
jokes.punchline: Jeannie's precise breakdown of time allocation revealing the relationship problem
jokes.explanation: Perfectly encapsulates the show's theme - precise mathematical breakdown of emotional neglect. Economy and insight combined

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: They get you for 8 minutes, and I get you for 90 seconds. That's pretty much the problem in a nutshell.

jokes.text: Isn't this a good crowd? - A beautiful crowd. - Isn't this a good crowd? - A beautiful crowd.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about the crowd quality
jokes.punchline: Exact repetition of the exchange showing their rote performance
jokes.explanation: Shows the mechanical nature of their on-air banter - repetitive and hollow

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Isn't this a good crowd?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: A beautiful crowd.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Isn't this a good crowd?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: A beautiful crowd.

jokes.text: No, my show is your footrest. Think of it that way.
jokes.setup: Catherine O'Hara apologizing for putting feet up
jokes.punchline: Larry's self-deprecating response about his show being a footrest
jokes.explanation: Good host recovery with self-deprecation - makes guest comfortable while getting a laugh

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, my show is your footrest. Think of it that way.

jokes.text: We basically want to kill him, I think.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Home Alone parents losing kid again
jokes.punchline: Catherine's dark suggestion they want to kill the child
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy escalation - takes the premise to its logical extreme with good economy

dialogue.speaker: Catherine O'Hara
dialogue.line: We basically want to kill him, I think.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, you said it twice there.
jokes.setup: Larry calling Catherine's performance 'effervescent' - 'Only one word: Effervescent'
jokes.punchline: Hank's literal correction pointing out Larry said it twice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank - missing the point entirely with pedantic literalism during emotional crisis

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, you said it twice there.

jokes.text: If they cross two or more time zones, that's a rule of thumb. Probably gone for good.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Jeannie going to Chicago
jokes.punchline: Artie's matter-of-fact relationship advice based on geography
jokes.explanation: Great Artie wisdom - practical, cynical relationship advice delivered with deadpan certainty

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: If they cross two or more time zones, that's a rule of thumb. Probably gone for good.

jokes.text: The last time you met him for the first time was at Chevy Chase's party.
jokes.setup: Larry asking if Catherine gets along with her husband
jokes.punchline: Her observation about Larry repeatedly meeting her husband 'for the first time'
jokes.explanation: Nice observation about Larry's self-absorption - not remembering people he's met multiple times

dialogue.speaker: Catherine O'Hara
dialogue.line: The last time you met him for the first time was at Chevy Chase's party.

jokes.text: Do you get along with him? - Bo? - No, Chevy. - No. - Neither do I.
jokes.setup: Context confusion about who Larry is asking about
jokes.punchline: Both agreeing they don't get along with Chevy Chase
jokes.explanation: Nice callback with misdirection - bonding over mutual Chevy Chase dislike

dialogue.line: Do you get along with him?

dialogue.speaker: Catherine O'Hara
dialogue.line: Bo?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, Chevy.

dialogue.speaker: Catherine O'Hara
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Neither do I.

jokes.text: I'm fucked.
jokes.setup: Catherine saying he balances career and home life perfectly
jokes.punchline: Larry's blunt, immediate contradiction
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and honesty - cuts through politeness with brutal self-assessment

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm fucked.

jokes.text: The show is a torturous, miserable hell.
jokes.setup: Larry dealing with marriage crisis during show
jokes.punchline: His dramatic assessment of the show's quality
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Larry's tendency to catastrophize under pressure

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: The show is a torturous, miserable hell.

jokes.text: There's no need to protect him. This month is normal.
jokes.setup: Hank saying this has been a tough month for Larry
jokes.punchline: Jeannie's cutting observation that this dysfunction is normal
jokes.explanation: Sharp character insight - Jeannie cutting through Hank's excuse-making with reality

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: There's no need to protect him. This month is normal.

jokes.text: Sure, he ignores you, but nothing in life is free.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to cheer up Jeannie about her marriage
jokes.punchline: His brutal honesty disguised as encouragement
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Hank logic - attempting comfort through acknowledging the problem. Perfect character-specific dysfunction

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Sure, he ignores you, but nothing in life is free.

jokes.text: One time they were doing it, he slipped up and called her 'Emmy.'
jokes.setup: Hank talking about friend married to Jerry Van Dyke who cares only about awards
jokes.punchline: During sex, he called his wife 'Emmy'
jokes.explanation: Good setup/punchline structure - plays on showbiz obsession bleeding into personal life

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: One time they were doing it, he slipped up and called her 'Emmy.'

jokes.text: He's the star of the show, Paula. - No duh, Beverly.
jokes.setup: Beverly stating the obvious about Larry being the star
jokes.punchline: Paula's sarcastic 'no duh' response
jokes.explanation: Basic workplace dynamic - Paula's impatience with Beverly's obvious observations

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: He's the star of the show, Paula.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: No duh, Beverly.

jokes.text: They're just women. They're projecting their own neuroses on you. They're all sick. You don't think they wish they were you?
jokes.setup: Artie trying to comfort Jeannie after she talked to the female staff
jokes.punchline: His casually misogynistic dismissal of the women as jealous
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate casual sexism from Artie - shows his old-school entertainment industry mindset

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: They're just women. They're projecting their own neuroses on you. They're all sick. You don't think they wish they were you?

jokes.text: Six weeks is her longest relationship except when she went on tour with Oingo Boingo. I think that was eight weeks.
jokes.setup: Artie describing Paula's dating history with musicians
jokes.punchline: The specific detail about Oingo Boingo tour being her longest relationship
jokes.explanation: Good specific detail - the Oingo Boingo reference adds specificity and shows Artie's gossipy knowledge

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Six weeks is her longest relationship except when she went on tour with Oingo Boingo. I think that was eight weeks.

jokes.text: Six weeks is her longest relationship... except when she went on tour with Oingo Boingo. I think that was eight weeks.
jokes.setup: Describing Paula's short relationships
jokes.punchline: Her longest relationship was following a band on tour
jokes.explanation: Good specific detail with Oingo Boingo reference. Character consistent for Hank's gossipy nature but not as sharp as the Copperfield joke.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Six weeks is her longest relationship... except when she went on tour with Oingo Boingo. I think that was eight weeks.

jokes.text: Do you know that I sleep all day? For the last two weeks all day, every day I'm asleep.
jokes.setup: Conversation about Jeannie's problems
jokes.punchline: Her revelation about sleeping all day as depression symptom
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy from depression - the repetitive phrasing emphasizes the obsessive nature

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: Do you know that I sleep all day? For the last two weeks all day, every day I'm asleep.

jokes.text: You should try to think of a performer as a small, helpless child. No, Artie. I have sex with him.
jokes.setup: Artie's advice to think of Larry as a helpless child
jokes.punchline: Jeannie's blunt sexual reality check
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection and timing - Jeannie cuts through Artie's metaphor with uncomfortable reality. Perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You should try to think of a performer as a small, helpless child.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: No, Artie. I have sex with him.

jokes.text: This is for the life story of Pavarotti. I'm playing him... No, I play him as an infant. And what did he weigh back then, would you suppose? He was about 185.

jokes.punchline: Playing him as infant who weighed 185 pounds
jokes.explanation: Good escalating absurdity - the infant Pavarotti weighing 185 pounds is a strong visual

dialogue.line: This is for the life story of Pavarotti. I'm playing him... No, I play him as an infant. And what did he weigh back then, would you suppose? He was about 185.

jokes.punchline: Billy suggesting the opera singer was already huge as an infant
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the baby Pavarotti bit. Works well as guest-host collaboration.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: And what did he weigh back then, would you suppose?

jokes.text: How's Jack Palance? Last time I saw him, he was swimming the Atlantic, I think.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about Jack Palance
jokes.punchline: Billy's absurd image of Palance swimming the Atlantic
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Jack Palance's tough-guy persona to absurd extremes

dialogue.speaker: Larry

dialogue.speaker: Billy Crystal
dialogue.line: Last time I saw him, he was swimming the Atlantic, I think.

jokes.text: You're asking all of this old stuff. 'You look marvelous'? Did I ask you to do the 'you look marvelous'? Jack Palance? That was last year.
jokes.setup: Billy confronting Larry about asking for old material
jokes.punchline: His frustration about being asked to repeat past bits
jokes.explanation: Meta commentary on talk show dynamics - performer frustration with host asking for old catchphrases

dialogue.speaker: Billy Crystal
dialogue.line: You're asking all of this old stuff. 'You look marvelous'? Did I ask you to do the 'you look marvelous'? Jack Palance? That was last year.

jokes.text: You can't let your personal life bleed into the show. I have a lot on my mind, but I'm trying to do my job.
jokes.setup: Billy telling Larry not to let personal life affect the show
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts himself by listing his personal problems
jokes.explanation: Good irony - Billy immediately violates his own advice by venting his problems

dialogue.speaker: Billy Crystal
dialogue.line: You can't let your personal life bleed into the show. I have a lot on my mind, but I'm trying to do my job.

jokes.text: Don't tell me this story again. I'm so tired of hearing this story. I beg of you, don't put me through this again.
jokes.setup: Billy starting to tell his Saturday Night Live rejection story
jokes.punchline: Larry's increasingly desperate pleas not to hear it again
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Larry's exasperation with Billy's repeated anecdote

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Don't tell me this story again. I'm so tired of hearing this story. I beg of you, don't put me through this again.

jokes.text: This is like the Hindenburg. When will you start shouting, 'Oh, the humanity'?
jokes.setup: Artie saying the show is going great while Larry's life falls apart
jokes.punchline: Larry comparing it to the Hindenburg disaster
jokes.explanation: Good historical reference with appropriate catastrophic scale - Larry's tendency to dramatize

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: This is like the Hindenburg. When will you start shouting, 'Oh, the humanity'?

jokes.text: Maybe. That's up to you. But all I can tell you is that we've got half the anniversary show right here!
jokes.setup: Larry asking if he should have marital fights every night for the show
jokes.punchline: Artie's enthusiasm about using the marriage crisis for anniversary show content
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie - turning personal tragedy into show business opportunity. Dark and character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Maybe. That's up to you. But all I can tell you is that we've got half the anniversary show right here!

jokes.text: It's sweeps. We have Schwarzenegger, Roseanne without the husband. We also have Julia Roberts. We have Cher, Aerosmith, the US Olympic basketball stars.
jokes.setup: Discussion of booking vacation during sweeps week
jokes.punchline: Beverly's impressive list of A-list bookings
jokes.explanation: List comedy showing the stakes - emphasizes how inappropriate vacation timing would be

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: It's sweeps. We have Schwarzenegger, Roseanne without the husband. We also have Julia Roberts. We have Cher, Aerosmith, the US Olympic basketball stars.

jokes.text: You wouldn't catch black folks doing this. The man takes charge. And the woman gets what she wants without making a fuss.
jokes.setup: Chaotic scene of Larry's marriage crisis affecting the show
jokes.punchline: Paula's cultural commentary on relationship dynamics
jokes.explanation: Cultural observation comedy - Paula's outside perspective on the dysfunction

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: You wouldn't catch black folks doing this. The man takes charge. And the woman gets what she wants without making a fuss.

jokes.text: It's sort of like a Raging Jew.
jokes.setup: Billy describing his movie about a Jewish comedian's career
jokes.punchline: Comparing it to 'Raging Bull' but for Jewish comedians
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay with cultural specificity - clever film reference adaptation

dialogue.speaker: Billy Crystal
dialogue.line: It's sort of like a Raging Jew.

jokes.text: It's very much his relationship... four, five, six... with his brother.
jokes.setup: Billy describing his movie's plot
jokes.punchline: Counting off during the interview, showing he's following drum cues
jokes.explanation: Nice meta moment showing the mechanics of talk show performance bleeding into the content. Good callback to earlier drummer mention.

dialogue.speaker: Billy Crystal
dialogue.line: It's very much his relationship... four, five, six... with his brother.

jokes.text: We lost the clip.
jokes.setup: Larry setting up Billy's movie clip
jokes.punchline: Flat announcement that they lost the clip
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - technical failure delivered with deadpan acceptance

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: We lost the clip.

jokes.text: Hi, Mom. How's Chicago?
jokes.setup: Larry bringing Jeannie on air to resolve their fight publicly
jokes.punchline: His awkward reference to her threat to go to Chicago
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe - Larry addressing their private fight on live television with forced casualness

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Hi, Mom. How's Chicago?

jokes.text: Well, honey, next week is sweeps week. I don't want to bump any big guests. Wait a minute. Kim Basinger. Good-bye. Hello, Jeannie Sanders. Tuesday night.
jokes.setup: Larry offering Jeannie a spot on the show next week
jokes.punchline: His immediate decision to bump Kim Basinger for his wife
jokes.explanation: Great escalation - Larry's desperate gesture to save marriage by bumping A-list guest

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, honey, next week is sweeps week. I don't want to bump any big guests. Wait a minute. Kim Basinger. Good-bye. Hello, Jeannie Sanders. Tuesday night.

jokes.text: Kim Basinger. Good-bye. Hello, Jeannie Sanders. Tuesday night.
jokes.setup: Checking the schedule for next week
jokes.punchline: Immediately bumping A-list actress for his wife
jokes.explanation: Great escalation showing Larry's misguided attempt to fix things. The casual dismissal of Kim Basinger is perfectly Larry.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Kim Basinger. Good-bye. Hello, Jeannie Sanders. Tuesday night.

jokes.text: Kim Basinger can do maintenance that day.
jokes.setup: Larry bumping Kim Basinger for Jeannie
jokes.punchline: His dismissive suggestion that Kim Basinger can do 'maintenance' instead
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up - Larry's casual dismissal of major movie star shows his priorities

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Kim Basinger can do maintenance that day.

jokes.text: Delightful! This is the best show we've ever done!
jokes.setup: Entire show has been a disaster with personal crisis bleeding through
jokes.punchline: Artie's enthusiastic assessment of the trainwreck as great television
jokes.explanation: Perfect final moment - Artie's producer instincts celebrating authentic dysfunction as entertainment. Brilliant character consistency and ironic capper.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Delightful! This is the best show we've ever done!

jokes.text: This is the best show we've ever done!

jokes.punchline: Artie's enthusiastic assessment that it's their best show
